population: 64 incident HA-ILI cases among 21,417 patients at risk
study_design: Prospective observational study with Bayesian modeling
hospital_acquired_definition: HA-ILI during hospital outbreaks
risk_factor_definition: Attributable fraction of HA-ILI due to patient exposure
finding_text: 59% of patient HA-ILI was attributable to exposure to contagious patients
key_limitations: HA-ILI rather than PCR-confirmed influenza
study_reported_implication: Patients were the dominant recorded source of exposure
codeblue_translation: Use as transmission-context support rather than core predictor

population: 64 incident HA-ILI cases among 21,417 patients at risk
study_design: Prospective observational study with Bayesian modeling
hospital_acquired_definition: HA-ILI during hospital outbreaks
risk_factor_definition: Attributable fraction of HA-ILI due to HCW exposure
finding_text: 41% of patient HA-ILI was attributable to exposure to contagious HCWs
key_limitations: HA-ILI rather than PCR-confirmed influenza
study_reported_implication: HCWs were a major recorded source of exposure
codeblue_translation: Use as staff-vector transmission context

population: 64 incident HA-ILI cases among 21,417 patients at risk
study_design: Prospective observational study with Bayesian modeling
hospital_acquired_definition: HA-ILI during hospital outbreaks
risk_factor_definition: Transmission linked to exposure one day earlier
finding_text: About 80% of HA-ILI was associated with exposure one day earlier
key_limitations: Model-based estimate
study_reported_implication: Secondary cases may appear as soon as the day after detection
codeblue_translation: Update transmission logic to emphasize short-lag spread

population: 64 incident HA-ILI cases among 21,417 patients at risk
study_design: Prospective observational study with Bayesian modeling
hospital_acquired_definition: HA-ILI during hospital outbreaks
risk_factor_definition: Transmission during unobservable contagious period of source patients
finding_text: 49% of patient-source HA-ILI occurred during unobservable contagiousness
key_limitations: Model-based estimate
study_reported_implication: Hidden patient transmission was substantial
codeblue_translation: Account for presymptomatic patient spread

population: 64 incident HA-ILI cases among 21,417 patients at risk
study_design: Prospective observational study with Bayesian modeling
hospital_acquired_definition: HA-ILI during hospital outbreaks
risk_factor_definition: Transmission during unobservable contagious period of source HCWs
finding_text: 82% of HCW-source HA-ILI occurred during unobservable contagiousness
key_limitations: Model-based estimate
study_reported_implication: Hidden HCW transmission was substantial
codeblue_translation: Account for presymptomatic HCW spread

population: 152 HCWs and 542 inpatients over 2 influenza seasons
study_design: Prospective surveillance study with sequencing/contact tracing
hospital_acquired_definition: Influenza infection in acute care during study monitoring
risk_factor_definition: Influenza-positive shedding without symptoms
finding_text: Influenza-positive swabs were collected on days without symptoms in both HCWs and inpatients
key_limitations: Study population was volunteer sample
study_reported_implication: Asymptomatic shedding exists in hospital influenza
codeblue_translation: Use as nuance in transmission interpretation

population: 152 HCWs and 542 inpatients over 2 influenza seasons
study_design: Prospective surveillance study with sequencing/contact tracing
hospital_acquired_definition: Influenza infection in acute care during study monitoring
risk_factor_definition: Observed transmission event from symptomatic case
finding_text: One monitored transmission event originated from a symptomatic case
key_limitations: Low number of observed events
study_reported_implication: Observed transmission in this study came from symptomatic shedding
codeblue_translation: Support symptom-triggered barrier precautions

population: 152 HCWs and 542 inpatients over 2 influenza seasons
study_design: Prospective surveillance study with sequencing/contact tracing
hospital_acquired_definition: Influenza infection in acute care during study monitoring
risk_factor_definition: No monitored transmission from asymptomatic positive individuals
finding_text: No transmission event occurred in monitored contacts with asymptomatic influenza-positive individuals
key_limitations: Absence of evidence, not proof of no transmission
study_reported_implication: Asymptomatic shedding did not generate observed transmission in monitored contacts
codeblue_translation: Use as nuance, not as absolute rule

population: 152 HCWs and 542 inpatients over 2 influenza seasons
study_design: Prospective surveillance study with sequencing/contact tracing
hospital_acquired_definition: Influenza infection in acute care during study monitoring
risk_factor_definition: Secondary attack rate in monitored acute-care setting
finding_text: Secondary attack rate was low
key_limitations: Low-event setting
study_reported_implication: Barrier precautions and vaccination may have limited transmission
codeblue_translation: Use as contextual baseline in simulation layer

population: 82 participants: 35 patients, 32 nurses/paramedical staff, 15 doctors
hospital_acquired_definition: Confirmed influenza in geriatric short-stay unit
risk_factor_definition: 3x3 role-based transmission matrix
finding_text: The study estimated a 3x3 role-based transmission matrix using individual contact data
key_limitations: Unit-specific modeling study
study_reported_implication: Role-based transmission is heterogeneous
codeblue_translation: Model spread edges by role rather than assuming uniform mixing

population: 292 hospitalized influenza patients; 28 hospital-acquired cases
hospital_acquired_definition: Symptom onset ≥48 h after admission unrelated to respiratory illness, or diagnosis ≥7 days if onset unknown
risk_factor_definition: Distinct hospital-acquired case clusters
finding_text: Cases occurred in two distinct clusters suggesting intra-hospital transmission
key_limitations: Single season at one center
study_reported_implication: Clusters support non-random intra-hospital spread
codeblue_translation: Use clustering as an outbreak escalation signal

population: 73,859 adults included after exclusions; 111 HAI cases
hospital_acquired_definition: Positive influenza A or B PCR >4 days after admission
risk_factor_definition: Same-room exposure to a patient with influenza
finding_text: Same-room exposure to an influenza patient was much more frequent in HAI patients
key_limitations: Observational comparison only; single-center study
study_reported_implication: Same-room overlap is a strong transmission-context signal
codeblue_translation: Use same-room exposure as a direct outbreak-risk feature

population: 73,859 adults included after exclusions; 111 HAI cases
hospital_acquired_definition: Positive influenza A or B PCR >4 days after admission
risk_factor_definition: Same-unit exposure to a patient with influenza
finding_text: Same-unit exposure to an influenza patient was much more frequent in HAI patients
key_limitations: Observational comparison only; single-center study
study_reported_implication: Same-unit overlap is a strong ward-level transmission signal
codeblue_translation: Use same-unit exposure as a ward-risk transmission feature

population: 73,859 adults included after exclusions; 111 HAI cases
hospital_acquired_definition: Positive influenza A or B PCR >4 days after admission
risk_factor_definition: Double-room occupancy as transmission context
finding_text: Double-room occupancy was the most influential predictor in the random-forest model and the authors interpret persistent close proximity in double rooms as facilitating transmission
key_limitations: No adjusted effect estimate; interpretation partly mechanistic
study_reported_implication: Double-room structure likely increases transmission opportunity
codeblue_translation: Use double-room placement as a structural transmission modifier

population: 298 inpatient contacts exposed to influenza from 11 index cases; 23 secondary cases
study_design: Prospective observational contact-follow-up study with PCR confirmation and sequencing subset
hospital_acquired_definition: Exposure defined as sharing a bay/room with a laboratory-confirmed case before isolation precautions
risk_factor_definition: Symptomatic PCR-confirmed influenza acquisition among inpatients exposed in a shared bay/room to a known case
finding_text: 23 of 298 contacts (7.7%) became secondary cases; among contacts completing follow-up, 23 of 105 (21.9%) became secondary cases
clinical_context: Adult inpatients exposed to influenza case in shared bay/room
key_limitations: Only symptomatic contacts were tested and many contacts were discharged before full follow-up
study_reported_implication: Inpatient bay/room exposure created a measurable nosocomial secondary-attack rate
codeblue_translation: Use shared-bay exposure as a direct transmission-risk signal

setting: Respiratory department of a 1520-bed tertiary hospital, Guangzhou
population: 8 influenza-like illness patients during respiratory-ward outbreak; 7 RT-PCR-confirmed A(H1N1)pdm09
study_design: Prospective outbreak investigation with RT-PCR, culture and phylogenetics
hospital_acquired_definition: ILI developing >48 h after hospitalization in the respiratory department
diagnostic_method: Rapid antigen test, RT-PCR, culture; sequencing subset
risk_factor_definition: Spatial clustering of nosocomial A(H1N1)pdm09 cases across rooms in a respiratory ward
finding_text: After the index case in room 11, eight patients in three rooms developed influenza-like illness before the control bundle contained the outbreak
key_limitations: Small single-ward outbreak
study_reported_implication: Room-level clustering reflected local nosocomial spread
codeblue_translation: Use same-room and nearby-room clustering as outbreak-escalation logic

setting: Respiratory department of a 1520-bed tertiary hospital, Guangzhou
population: 8 influenza-like illness patients during respiratory-ward outbreak; 7 RT-PCR-confirmed A(H1N1)pdm09
study_design: Prospective outbreak investigation with RT-PCR, culture and phylogenetics
hospital_acquired_definition: ILI developing >48 h after hospitalization in the respiratory department
diagnostic_method: Rapid antigen test, RT-PCR, culture; sequencing subset
risk_factor_definition: Prolonged RT-PCR positivity in outbreak case with bronchiectasis and Penicillium marneffei infection
finding_text: One patient remained RT-PCR-positive 11 days after fever onset, indicating prolonged viral shedding beyond fever duration
key_limitations: Single-case observation within a small outbreak
study_reported_implication: Immunocompromised or structurally abnormal-lung patients may shed longer
codeblue_translation: Flag prolonged-shedding hosts as extended transmission-risk nodes

population: 94158 influenza-associated hospitalizations; 353 HAI cases
hospital_acquired_definition: Positive influenza test date and respiratory symptom onset >3 days after admission; possible HAI if positive test >3 days after admission with missing symptom-onset date
risk_factor_definition: Population prevalence and rate of hospital-acquired influenza among influenza-associated hospitalizations
finding_text: Among 94158 influenza-associated hospitalizations, 353 (0.4%) were classified as hospital-acquired influenza; adjusted rate was 0.4 per 100000 persons, increasing to 1.3% prevalence and 1.5 per 100000 when possible HAI cases were included
key_limitations: Testing was not systematic and HAI was likely underestimated
study_reported_implication: HAI rates were low but likely underestimated
codeblue_translation: Use as baseline surveillance burden context, not as patient-level risk

setting: Inpatient hematology and allogeneic stem cell transplant unit
population: All admitted patients during H1N1 outbreak window; 12 outbreak-associated influenza A cases
hospital_acquired_definition: Nosocomial outbreak window from 1 week before index case until 4 weeks after prophylaxis implementation
diagnostic_method: PCR on nasopharyngeal swabs and/or bronchoalveolar lavage
comparator_group: Outbreak before vs after universal prophylaxis
risk_factor_definition: Universal oseltamivir prophylaxis for all inpatients during recognized ward outbreak
finding_text: After clustering of seven cases triggered recognition of the outbreak, universal oseltamivir prophylaxis plus reinforced infection control was followed by no further influenza A cases linked to the outbreak
key_limitations: Single outbreak in a highly specialized unit
study_reported_implication: Early universal prophylaxis appeared effective in controlling spread in this setting
codeblue_translation: Use as outbreak-control support for high-risk units when clustered transmission is recognized

setting: Hypothetical general hospital in Ann Arbor, Michigan
population: Hospital patients and healthcare workers interacting with community
hospital_acquired_definition: Not applicable; simulated hospital transmission
risk_factor_definition: Improved hand-washing compliance as an influenza control measure
finding_text: In the simulation, hand-washing was the most effective individual strategy and reduced hospital influenza cases by 11%-27%
key_limitations: Simulation study rather than empirical ward data
study_reported_implication: Hand hygiene was the strongest single modeled intervention
codeblue_translation: Use as policy-supporting transmission evidence, not as a direct empirical effect size

setting: Hypothetical general hospital in Ann Arbor, Michigan
population: Hospital patients and healthcare workers interacting with community
hospital_acquired_definition: Not applicable; simulated hospital transmission
risk_factor_definition: Healthcare-worker influenza vaccination as an influenza control measure
finding_text: In the simulation, healthcare-worker vaccination reduced hospital influenza cases by 6%-19%
key_limitations: Simulation study rather than empirical ward data
study_reported_implication: HCW vaccination contributed meaningfully to reduced transmission
codeblue_translation: Use as policy-supporting evidence for staff-focused prevention

setting: Hypothetical general hospital in Ann Arbor, Michigan
population: Hospital patients and healthcare workers interacting with community
hospital_acquired_definition: Not applicable; simulated hospital transmission
risk_factor_definition: Prevaccination of patients before exposure as an influenza control measure
finding_text: In the simulation, patient prevaccination reduced hospital influenza cases by 4%-17%
key_limitations: Simulation study rather than empirical ward data
study_reported_implication: Prevaccination of patients contributed to lower transmission
codeblue_translation: Use as supportive evidence for pre-season patient vaccination planning

setting: Hypothetical general hospital in Ann Arbor, Michigan
population: Hospital patients and healthcare workers interacting with community
hospital_acquired_definition: Not applicable; simulated hospital transmission
risk_factor_definition: Patient isolation as an influenza control measure
finding_text: In the simulation, patient isolation reduced hospital influenza cases by 5%-16%
key_limitations: Simulation study rather than empirical ward data
study_reported_implication: Isolation was a meaningful component of transmission control
codeblue_translation: Use as supportive evidence for isolation/cohorting logic

setting: Hypothetical general hospital in Ann Arbor, Michigan
population: Hospital patients and healthcare workers interacting with community
hospital_acquired_definition: Not applicable; simulated hospital transmission
risk_factor_definition: Antiviral treatment as an influenza control measure
finding_text: In the simulation, antiviral treatment reduced hospital influenza cases by 4%-14%
key_limitations: Simulation study rather than empirical ward data
study_reported_implication: Antiviral treatment contributed to lower hospital transmission
codeblue_translation: Use as supportive evidence for treatment/prophylaxis review logic

setting: Hypothetical general hospital in Ann Arbor, Michigan
population: Hospital patients and healthcare workers interacting with community
hospital_acquired_definition: Not applicable; simulated hospital transmission
risk_factor_definition: Use of face masks as an influenza control measure
finding_text: In the simulation, face masks reduced hospital influenza cases by 3%-10%
key_limitations: Simulation study rather than empirical ward data
study_reported_implication: Face masks had a modest but meaningful modeled effect
codeblue_translation: Use as supportive evidence for masking in bundled prevention

setting: Hypothetical general hospital in Ann Arbor, Michigan
population: Hospital patients and healthcare workers interacting with community
hospital_acquired_definition: Not applicable; simulated hospital transmission
risk_factor_definition: Combined implementation of all modeled infection control strategies
finding_text: Use of all strategies together could potentially halve observed hospital influenza cases under ideal compliance and achieve almost 40% reduction under a more realistic scenario
key_limitations: Simulation study rather than empirical ward data
study_reported_implication: A multifaceted approach was clearly superior to single measures
codeblue_translation: Use as support for bundled rather than single-intervention policy logic

population: 4110 hospitalized adult PCR-positive influenza patients; 430 HAI cases
study_design: Prospective multi-season observational study with subtype analysis
hospital_acquired_definition: Medical-record review used to confirm nosocomial origin
comparator_group: H3N2 vs H1N1pdm09 vs influenza B
risk_factor_definition: Influenza subtype as determinant of hospital dissemination
finding_text: Influenza A(H3N2) infections were more often hospital-acquired (15.1%) than A(H1N1)pdm09 (6.3%) and influenza B (6.8%), and 73.3% of H3N2 HAI cases were clustered, accounting for all 20 hospital outbreaks with >=4 affected patients
key_limitations: Observational design
study_reported_implication: H3N2 was associated with markedly greater hospital dissemination
codeblue_translation: Use subtype information, especially H3N2, to intensify outbreak vigilance

population: 222 exposed adult inpatients who could not be immediately separated from index cases
hospital_acquired_definition: Exposure to influenza in hospital setting without immediate separation
diagnostic_method: Clinical follow-up and influenza incidence assessment after prophylaxis
risk_factor_definition: Five-day versus 10-day postexposure prophylaxis with oseltamivir after hospital exposure
finding_text: In intention-to-treat analysis, influenza incidence was 1.8% in the 5-day group and 0% in the 10-day group, and 5-day prophylaxis was noninferior to the 10-day regimen
key_limitations: Single-center study
study_reported_implication: Five-day prophylaxis appeared sufficient in this exposure scenario
codeblue_translation: Use as support for shorter postexposure prophylaxis review when immediate separation is not feasible

population: 1559 hospital stays among 1540 adults with positive influenza tests over 5 epidemic seasons
hospital_acquired_definition: Nosocomial influenza over consecutive epidemic seasons
comparator_group: 5-measure units vs baseline season and 3-measure units
risk_factor_definition: Five-modality prevention strategy including HCW vaccination promotion, surveillance, communication, improved diagnosis, and systematic surgical mask use
finding_text: In units implementing five measures, nosocomial influenza risk was reduced over subsequent seasons compared with 2014-2015, with significant reductions in 2016-2017 (IRR 0.39) and 2018-2019 (IRR 0.48), whereas no significant difference was seen in units implementing only three measures
key_limitations: Retrospective ecological implementation study
study_reported_implication: A broader multimodal strategy, especially with rapid diagnosis and mask use, appeared to halve risk in high-risk units
codeblue_translation: Use as support for enhanced bundled prevention in wards receiving many influenza patients

population: Network-wide hospital surveillance during universal masking period
hospital_acquired_definition: Nosocomial respiratory viral infection defined as onset of fever or respiratory symptoms >48 hours after admission
diagnostic_method: Xpert Xpress Flu/RSV and hospital surveillance
risk_factor_definition: Universal masking for healthcare workers and patients in hospitals
finding_text: With 100% compliance in healthcare workers and 75.9% in patients, zero rates of nosocomial influenza A, influenza B, and RSV were achieved from February to April 2020, significantly lower than corresponding months in 2017-2019
key_limitations: The study occurred during a broader COVID-era infection-control bundle, so masking was not isolated as the only cause
study_reported_implication: Universal masking was associated with disappearance of nosocomial influenza during the observation period
codeblue_translation: Use as strong supportive evidence for universal masking in high-risk periods

population: 464 hospitalized influenza patients during 2017-2018 epidemic season
hospital_acquired_definition: HAI occurrence after co-hospitalization with laboratory-confirmed influenza case
risk_factor_definition: Emergency strategy for placing influenza patients in multiple-bed rooms using non-immunocompromised neighbor selection, physical barriers, masking and pre-emptive oseltamivir
finding_text: Among exposed neighbors, eight matched HAI definition but seven were already exposed before strategy set-up; only one documented transmission occurred after incorrect application of the strategy due to wrong oseltamivir prophylactic dosing
key_limitations: No systematic monitoring of neighbors; detection bias acknowledged
study_reported_implication: Maximum precautions may limit HAI even in multiple-bed rooms
codeblue_translation: Use as contingency evidence for crisis-capacity multi-bed placement with strict safeguards

setting: Adult cystic fibrosis center with dedicated ward
population: 21 inpatients during outbreak; 10 developed influenza B
study_design: Outbreak investigation with clinical and environmental review
hospital_acquired_definition: Outbreak of influenza B on specialist ward with strict infection control procedures
diagnostic_method: Respiratory viral PCR plus environmental ventilation review
risk_factor_definition: Room proximity and inadequate room ventilation as contributors to outbreak spread
finding_text: Attack rate was 48% (10/21); nine affected rooms were in close proximity while the two isolation rooms with enhanced ventilation had no infections, and affected rooms had 1.75-2.10 air changes per hour, below recommendations
key_limitations: Single outbreak; environmental association rather than causal proof
study_reported_implication: Poor ventilation and ward layout likely contributed to spread
codeblue_translation: Use ventilation and room-layout signals in outbreak-risk assessment

population: 57 nosocomial influenza cases identified during one influenza season
study_design: Retrospective study of documented nosocomial influenza
hospital_acquired_definition: Positive PCR >=48 h after admission with no influenza-like symptoms during the first 48 h
risk_factor_definition: Acquisition context in single versus double rooms
finding_text: Nosocomial influenza was mostly acquired in double-bedded rooms, accounting for 39 of 57 cases (68.4%)
key_limitations: Descriptive association without matched room-level comparator
study_reported_implication: Shared rooms appeared to be a common setting for acquisition
codeblue_translation: Use double-room exposure as a structural transmission-context signal

population: 57 nosocomial influenza cases and 803 community-acquired cases during one influenza season
hospital_acquired_definition: Positive PCR >=48 h after admission with no influenza-like symptoms during the first 48 h
risk_factor_definition: Over-representation of influenza B among nosocomial cases
finding_text: Influenza B accounted for 80.7% of nosocomial cases versus 44.6% of community-acquired cases
key_limitations: Single-center single-season study
study_reported_implication: Influenza B may have had higher transmissibility and/or been associated with transmission clusters
codeblue_translation: Use influenza B seasons as higher-alert periods for hospital dissemination

population: 16273 respiratory virus assays over three pre-COVID seasons
hospital_acquired_definition: Positive test on or after hospital day 4 considered putatively hospital-acquired
diagnostic_method: Respiratory viral panel and influenza/RSV PCR
risk_factor_definition: Influenza A/B burden and clustering within mixed respiratory surveillance
finding_text: The frequency of putatively hospital-acquired infection was 6.9% for influenza A/B, and among 176 respiratory virus clusters, 62 were associated with influenza A/B
key_limitations: Mixed-virus study rather than influenza-only cohort
study_reported_implication: Influenza accounted for a substantial share of identified transmission clusters
codeblue_translation: Use as supplementary surveillance evidence for influenza clustering in acute care

population: 27 hospital wards over 10 years; adult patients tested for respiratory viruses
study_design: Retrospective cohort on ward-year aggregated data
hospital_acquired_definition: Positive PCR performed at least 5 days after admission defined HA-VRI
risk_factor_definition: Proportion of double-occupancy rooms within a ward
finding_text: After adjustment, incidence of HA-VRI increased by 0.04 per 1000 hospital-days for each 10% increase in double-occupancy rooms, and a similar association remained in the influenza subgroup
key_limitations: This was a mixed respiratory-virus study, although the influenza subgroup showed similar findings
study_reported_implication: Double-occupancy rooms independently increased ward-level hospital-acquired viral respiratory infection incidence
codeblue_translation: Use proportion of double rooms as a structural outbreak-risk modifier

population: 27 hospital wards over 10 years; adult patients tested for respiratory viruses
study_design: Retrospective cohort on ward-year aggregated data
hospital_acquired_definition: Positive PCR performed at least 5 days after admission defined HA-VRI
risk_factor_definition: Incidence of community-acquired viral respiratory infections among patients admitted to the ward
finding_text: After adjustment, HA-VRI incidence increased by 0.10 per 1000 hospital-days for each additional point of community-acquired VRI incidence in the ward
key_limitations: Mixed respiratory-virus analysis rather than influenza-only
study_reported_implication: Higher ward importation pressure from community cases increased hospital-acquired respiratory virus incidence
codeblue_translation: Use incoming community-case pressure as an outbreak-dynamics modifier

setting: Two acute care hospitals in Bern
population: 764 at-risk patients hospitalized >=3 days in multi-bed rooms during DroPS implementation
hospital_acquired_definition: Clinical HARVI monitored on wards using DroPS symptom-based bedside precautions
comparator_group: At-risk patients in multi-bed rooms under DroPS
risk_factor_definition: Symptom-based droplet precautions on-site at bedside in multi-bed rooms
finding_text: Among 764 at-risk patients in multi-bed rooms, 11 (1.4%) developed clinical HARVI and only 3 tested positive (0.4%; including 1 influenza), suggesting low transmission under DroPS
key_limitations: Mixed influenza/RSV study with few laboratory-confirmed events
study_reported_implication: DroPS may be a pragmatic alternative when single-room isolation is limited
codeblue_translation: Use as contingency evidence for symptom-based bedside droplet precautions

population: 1186 hospitalized influenza patients across five influenza seasons
hospital_acquired_definition: Nosocomial influenza incidence compared before vs after strict staff-mask intervention
risk_factor_definition: Ward-wide mandatory surgical mask use by staff during the full shift when at least three influenza patients were simultaneously present on a ward
finding_text: Before intervention, several dates had clusters of up to eight new nosocomial cases per day, whereas no such clusters were observed after strict staff masking was implemented; average nosocomial incidence fell from 0.39 to 0.19
key_limitations: Retrospective design with concurrent hand-hygiene improvement
study_reported_implication: Strict staff masking appeared to suppress clustered ward transmission
codeblue_translation: Add ward-triggered universal staff masking to outbreak-control logic

setting: Short-stay acute care for the elderly unit
population: 82 individuals: 35 patients, 32 nurses/paramedical staff, 15 medical doctors
study_design: Pilot stochastic transmission-model study using RFID contact data
hospital_acquired_definition: Nosocomial transmissibility modeled from documented individual contact patterns and confirmed influenza cases
risk_factor_definition: Who-acquires-infection-from-whom matrix derived from individual contact data and fitted transmission probabilities
finding_text: The modeled transmission matrix showed the strongest patient-ward transmission from nurses to patients (1.04/day), lower transmission from doctors to patients (0.38/day), and nurse-to-nurse transmission of 0.34/day
adjustment_level: SEIR modeling with observed contact data
key_limitations: Pilot single-unit modeling study
study_reported_implication: Transmission opportunity in hospitals is strongly role-dependent rather than homogeneous
codeblue_translation: Use role-specific contact structure in outbreak-dynamics reasoning, especially nurse-patient edges
tags: WHO ACQUIRES FROM WHOM; CONTACT MATRIX

setting: Short-stay acute care for the elderly unit
population: 82 individuals: 35 patients, 32 nurses/paramedical staff, 15 medical doctors
study_design: Pilot stochastic transmission-model study using RFID contact data
hospital_acquired_definition: Nosocomial transmissibility modeled from documented individual contact data and confirmed influenza cases
risk_factor_definition: Average daily contact burden between hospitalized patients and staff roles
finding_text: Based on RFID data, a hospitalized patient had an average of 22.17 contacts per day with nurses versus 5.85 with medical doctors, showing strongly asymmetric nurse-patient contact density
key_limitations: Pilot single-unit study with contact-based modeling
study_reported_implication: Transmission opportunity in the ward was concentrated on nurse-patient edges
codeblue_translation: Use high nurse-patient contact density as a mechanistic transmission-context signal

population: 1559 stays among 1540 hospitalized adults with positive influenza tests over five epidemic seasons
acquisition_context: Multimodal prevention implementation in high-risk units
hospital_acquired_definition: Nosocomial case defined as positive influenza sample with symptom onset at 72 h of hospitalization or later
risk_factor_definition: Adequate systematic surgical mask use by HCWs in risk units during the epidemic period
finding_text: Adequate HCW mask use in risk units increased from 73.6% in 2015-2016 to 85.7% in 2018-2019
clinical_context: Risk units receiving many influenza patients
key_limitations: Implementation metric rather than direct causal effect size
study_reported_implication: Surgical masks were well accepted by HCWs and adherence improved over time
codeblue_translation: Use mask-compliance monitoring as an outbreak-control implementation KPI

population: 1559 stays among 1540 hospitalized adults with positive influenza tests over five epidemic seasons
acquisition_context: Multimodal prevention implementation in high-risk units
hospital_acquired_definition: Nosocomial case defined as positive influenza sample with symptom onset at 72 h of hospitalization or later
comparator_group: Risk units vs non-risk units over implementation period
risk_factor_definition: Expansion of rapid RT-PCR and point-of-care influenza diagnosis in units receiving many influenza patients
finding_text: Rapid RT-PCR was available routinely in risk units from 2015-2016 onward, extended to risk units and ICUs from 2016-2017, and point-of-care testing became fully available in the emergency department in 2018-2019; the authors interpret faster diagnosis as likely shortening time to precautions
clinical_context: High-risk hospital units during influenza epidemics
key_limitations: No isolated per-measure causal estimate
study_reported_implication: Rapid diagnosis likely contributed to lower nosocomial transmission by reducing delay to precautions
codeblue_translation: Use rapid and point-of-care influenza diagnosis as a transmission-control enabler in high-risk units

population: 106 HCWs with influenza or influenza-like illness; 234 hospitalized patients exposed
study_design: Descriptive epidemiological study with questionnaire and chart review
hospital_acquired_definition: Exposed patients were identified after contact with HCWs with laboratory-confirmed influenza or ILI
risk_factor_definition: Antiviral chemoprophylaxis management after exposure to infected HCWs
finding_text: Of 234 exposed patients, 156 (66.7%) met criteria for antiviral chemoprophylaxis, 137 (87.3%) accepted, none had adverse events, and 2 developed laboratory-confirmed influenza after exposure
clinical_context: Staff-source exposure events in hospitalized patients
key_limitations: Included ILI as well as laboratory-confirmed influenza; descriptive design only
study_reported_implication: HCW-source exposure generated substantial downstream prophylaxis workload
codeblue_translation: Add post-exposure prophylaxis burden as a staff-vector response signal

population: 4110 hospitalized adult PCR-positive influenza patients; 430 HAI cases
study_design: Prospective multi-season observational study with subtype analysis
hospital_acquired_definition: Medical-record review used to confirm nosocomial origin
comparator_group: HAI H3N2 vs HAI H1N1 and HAI B
risk_factor_definition: Probability of finding additional linked hospital cases after identifying an initial HAI case of a given subtype
finding_text: The risk of subsequently finding additional cases after identifying an initial HAI H3N2 case was 40% versus 18% for H1N1 and 15% for influenza B; odds ratio for H3N2 was 2.4
adjustment_level: Subtype comparison within prospective observational data
key_limitations: Cluster linkage was epidemiologic rather than whole-genome-confirmed
study_reported_implication: H3N2 initial cases were much more likely to seed additional hospital spread
codeblue_translation: Use H3N2 subtype detection as a strong outbreak-escalation signal

population: 57 nosocomial influenza cases during one influenza season
study_design: Retrospective study of documented nosocomial influenza
hospital_acquired_definition: Positive PCR >=48 h after admission with no influenza-like symptoms during first 48 h
risk_factor_definition: Time between symptom onset and laboratory diagnosis of nosocomial influenza
finding_text: Median time from symptom onset to diagnosis was 29 hours, indicating a substantial window during which transmission could occur before recognition
key_limitations: Single-center, one-season study
study_reported_implication: Nosocomial influenza diagnosis was often delayed by more than one day
codeblue_translation: Use time-to-diagnosis as a hidden-spread vulnerability signal

setting: Two acute care hospitals in Bern
population: 764 at-risk patients hospitalized in multi-bed rooms under DroPS
hospital_acquired_definition: Clinical HARVI monitored on wards using DroPS symptom-based bedside precautions
risk_factor_definition: Hospital-acquired respiratory viral infection among patients exposed to infected neighbors while DroPS was in place
finding_text: No HARVI occurred in patients exposed to infected neighbours under the DroPS strategy
key_limitations: Mixed influenza/RSV study with small event numbers
study_reported_implication: DroPS may protect even in direct room-exposure situations
codeblue_translation: Use zero-neighbor-transmission under DroPS as supportive contingency evidence

population: 222 exposed adult inpatients who could not be immediately separated from index cases
hospital_acquired_definition: Exposure to influenza in hospital setting without immediate separation
diagnostic_method: Clinical follow-up and influenza incidence assessment after prophylaxis
risk_factor_definition: Impact of delayed physical separation after prophylaxis initiation when spatial constraints prevent immediate isolation
finding_text: In this hospital setting, a median delay of 2 days in separating exposed patients from index cases did not appear to compromise prophylaxis efficacy; overall transmission incidence remained low at 0.9%
key_limitations: Single-center trial; sequencing not performed
study_reported_implication: Once prophylaxis was started, short delay in physical separation did not seem to abolish protection
codeblue_translation: Use delayed-separation context as acceptable fallback logic when immediate isolation is impossible
